attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to her local emergency department with stridor. She was diagnosed with multiple system atrophy two years ago and reports progressive weakness affecting her breathing. She has no other diagnosed medical conditions and takes no regular medications. She lives at home with her partner and remains independent with activities of daily living.

On examination, she appears mildly distressed. Her pulse rate is 96/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 36.9°C. Her airways are patent with audible inspiratory stridor. There are no other abnormal respiratory findings.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with stridor in the context of multiple system atrophy (MSA), a progressive neurodegenerative condition. Stridor in MSA typically occurs due to vocal cord dysfunction and laryngeal muscle weakness, rather than airway inflammation or obstruction.

Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) is the most appropriate initial management. CPAP helps maintain upper airway patency, reduces work of breathing, and can provide significant symptomatic relief. It is a non-invasive intervention that aligns with the typically palliative approach to MSA management.

Nebulised salbutamol would not be effective in this situation as the stridor is due to upper airway dysfunction rather than bronchospasm. Beta-agonists act on bronchial smooth muscle and would not address vocal cord dysfunction.

Oral corticosteroids would not be effective as the stridor is due to neuromuscular dysfunction rather than inflammation. They may cause unnecessary side effects without providing benefit.

Referral for tracheostomy would be overly aggressive as an initial management step. While it might be considered in refractory cases, there is new data supporting its role in extending survival time compared to CPAP. Less invasive options should be tried first, and only following further discussion with patient should a surgical referral be considered.

Nebulised adrenaline is appropriate for acute inflammatory or infectious causes of stridor (such as croup) but would not address the underlying neurological cause in this case.

This presentation should also prompt discussion about advance care planning and management preferences for future respiratory complications, as stridor can be an indicator of disease progression in MSA. The goals of care should be established early, and a clear management plan developed in consultation with the patient and their family.
learningPoints: Stridor in progressive neurological disease often indicates upper airway dysfunction requiring urgent assessment.
Early discussion of management preferences and advance care planning is essential in progressive neurological conditions.
Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) can provide symptomatic relief for stridor in neurodegenerative conditions.
references: Goh YY, Saunders E, Pavey S, Rushton E, Quinn N, Houlden H, Chelban V. Multiple system atrophy. Practical Neurology. 2023 Jun 1;23(3):208-21. Available online ;https://pn.bmj.com/content/practneurol/23/3/208.full.pdf

Giannini G, Provini F, Cani I, Cecere A, Mignani F, Guaraldi P, Di Mirto CV, Cortelli P, Calandra‐Buonaura G. Tracheostomy is associated with increased survival in Multiple System Atrophy patients with stridor. European Journal of Neurology. 2022 Aug;29(8):2232-40. Available online at ; https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdfdirect/10.1111/ene.15347
